title: Benchmark for Quantum Teleportation with Non-Uniform Prior Distributions
authors: Tomáš Opatrný, Allison Brattley, Kunal K. Das
abstract: Quantum teleportation should surpass maximum fidelity thresholds possible with local measurements and classical communications. Benchmarks have been established when states are drawn from a uniform distribution of qubits or coherent states of harmonic oscillators. These are not applicable when there is prior knowledge about the input that skews the distribution. We determine the highest mean fidelity achievable for teleportation of states without using entanglement but drawn from a non-uniform prior distribution, specifically a von Mises - Fisher distribution. Using Bayesian methods, we derive benchmark functions for single qubit teleportation that utilize projective measurement on specific axes on the Bloch sphere. We demonstrate that similar results can be achieved with a coherent spin positive operator-valued measure (POVM), based upon heterodyne detection. These methods are then used to derive corresponding benchmarks for teleporting multi-qubit systems that we model as spin coherent states, covering the full range of particle number $N\in [1, \infty)$. The results show that fidelity thresholds to declare quantum advantage can be significantly higher than is typically assumed with uniform distribution.
summary: This paper shows that if you have prior knowledge about the states being teleported, the standard benchmarks for proving quantum advantage are too low. By using a von Mises-Fisher distribution, the authors derive new, higher fidelity thresholds that account for non-uniform input distributions.
main_problem: Establishing reliable fidelity benchmarks for quantum teleportation when input states are drawn from non-uniform prior distributions, rather than the standard uniform distribution.
main_result: The study demonstrates that standard fidelity thresholds (like 2/3 for qubits) can significantly underestimate the classical limit when prior knowledge exists, and that optimal teleportation strategies require specific measurement axes related to the distribution's concentration.
method: The authors use Bayesian inference and optimization to derive analytical expressions for mean fidelity, comparing projective measurements and Coherent Spin POVMs.
model_or_system: The model includes single qubits on the Bloch sphere and multi-qubit systems modeled as spin coherent states, with input states following a von Mises-Fisher distribution.
key_observables: Mean fidelity (average fidelity of the teleportation process).
important_parameters: Concentration parameter (kappa), mean number of excitations (n), and particle number (N).
main_assumptions: The results are derived specifically for spin coherent states and von Mises-Fisher distributions.
figures_summary: Figure 1 plots mean fidelity against mean excitations for different projection axes; Figure 2 shows the optimal estimated angle versus detected angle; Figure 3 compares different measurement strategies against the 2/3 baseline; Figure 4 shows multi-particle fidelity scaling.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of non-uniform priors, develops the mathematical framework using Bayesian methods for single and multi-qubit systems, compares measurement strategies (projective vs. POVM), and provides analytical derivations in the appendix.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for quantum optics and quantum information as it redefines the criteria for claiming 'quantum advantage' in realistic scenarios where input states are not perfectly unknown.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Mean ﬁdelity for no-entanglement teleportation of a single qubit using projection on diﬀerent axes θ0 as a function of the mean number of excitations ⟨n⟩of Eq. (3). The green dashed line corresponds to the “do-nothing” strategy as in Eq. (4). The red dotted line is teleportation using projection on the direction of prior knowledge, without averaging over the prior distribution. The top-most solid blue line corresponds to θ0 = π/2, and the lines move down in steps of π/16. The bottom solid blue line overlapping with the green dashed and red dotted lines corresponds to θ0 = 0.

FIG. 2. Optimum estimated angle ˜θ as a function of the de- tected angle θM for a single qubit POVM scheme, as in Eq. (16), for various distributions of prior knowledge character- ized by the values of ⟨n⟩. The dashed line corresponds to n0 ≃0.37, the turning point between monotonous increase and non-monotonous behavior.

FIG. 3. Comparison of the mean ﬁdelity for no-entanglement teleportation of a single qubit, with prior knowledge using POVM detection and projective measurement. The solid red line is the mean ﬁdelity using a POVM as in Eq. (19), and the blue dashed line uses projective measurement on σx as in Eq. (10). The inset displays the diﬀerence between the two lines. The “do-nothing” strategy is included as a green dashed line for comparison. The mean ﬁdelity limit of 2/3 for no prior distribution is marked as a thin black line.

FIG. 4. Maximum mean ﬁdelities achieved with no entangle- ment for diﬀerent qubit numbers N as a function of the mean number of excitations ⟨n⟩. The curves correspond to, from left to right, N = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 10, and N →∞, as given by Eqs. (B11) and (24), respectively.
